name: openqasm3

metadata.name: openqasm3
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License

documentation:
# OpenQASM 3 Python Reference

[![License](https://img.shields.io/github/license/openqasm/openqasm.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/Apache-2.0)<!-- long-description-skip-begin -->[![Release](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/openqasm3)](https://pypi.org/project/openqasm3)<!-- long-description-skip-end -->

The `openqasm3` package contains the reference abstract syntax tree (AST) for representing OpenQASM 3 programs, tools to parse text into this AST, and tools to manipulate the AST.

The AST is intended to help with writing compiler passes for OpenQASM 3 in Python.
It aims to have no dependencies for users who consume the Python tree structure, and minimal dependencies for parsing a string to this tree structure.
The AST is simpler than a Concrete Syntax Tree (CST) which preserves comments, spacing, etc for use by editor plugins.

The package consists of the modules:

* `openqasm3.ast`: The AST nodes.

* `openqasm3.parser`:
  A parser based on an ANTLR grammar and reference parser also found in this repo.
  It walks the ANTLR parse tree to generate the AST.

* `openqasm3.visitor`: A base AST visitor and transformer.

* `tests`: A set of unit tests.


**Note**: this reference Python package is currently in the early stages of development, and _no_ parts of the API should be considered stable at this time.
The AST itself will be subject to change in backwards-incompatible ways, mirroring the development of the OpenQASM 3 languge itself.


## Installation

The package can be installed from PyPI (`pip`) with the command

```bash
python -m pip install openqasm3
```

If you want to use the parser, you will need to install the extra `parser`, and to run the tests you need the extra `tests`, for example `pip install openqasm3[tests]`.
All extras can be installed with the target `openqasm3[all]`.


## Development Environment

To work on development, you will need to have a complete [ANTLR](https://www.antlr.org/) installation (not just the runtime), and the ANTLR grammar files from the [main OpenQASM repository](https://github.com/openqasm/openqasm).

### Setting up ANTLR

You can most likely get a copy of ANTLR using your system package manager if you are on Linux, or from [Homebrew](https://brew.sh) (`brew`) on macOS.
Otherwise, you can follow [these instructions](https://github.com/antlr/antlr4/blob/master/doc/getting-started.md).
Make a note of the version of ANTLR you have installed, because you will need to ensure your version of `antlr4-python3-runtime` matches up to the minor version.

Once you have ANTLR installed, change to the directory where the `qasm3*.g4` files are located (for example, `repo_root/source/grammar`), and run
```bash
<antlr command> -o <path to here>/openqasm3/_antlr/_<major>_<minor> -Dlanguage=Python3 -visitor qasm3Lexer.g4 qasm3Parser.g4
```

For example, if this repository is cloned to `~/openqasm` and the command to run ANTLR 4.11.1 is `antlr4`, then you should run
```bash
cd ~/openqasm/source/grammar
antlr4 -o ~/openqasm/source/openqasm/openqasm3/_antlr/_4_11 -Dlanguage=Python3 -visitor qasm3Lexer.g4 qasm3Parser.g4
```

You can install more than one version of the ANTLR files at once, provided you put them in the correct version directories.
The package will dynamically choose the correct version based on the installed version of `antlr4_python3_runtime` when it is imported.

### Developer tools

Install the rest of the Python development environment with
```bash
python -m pip install -r requirements.txt -r requirements-dev.txt
```
ensuring that the version of `antlr4-python3-runtime` exactly matches the version of ANTLR you have.

Install the Python package in editable mode with
```bash
python -m pip install -e .
```

The project is configured to use the code formatter [`black`](https://pypi.org/project/black), linter [`pylint`](https://pylint.org) and tester [`pytest`](https://pytest.org).
The commands to run these are, respectively:
```bash
black .
pylint .
pytest
```


### Deployment procedure

The deployment is primarily managed by a GitHub Actions pipeline, triggered by a tag of the form `ast-py/v<version>`.
For example, for package version `0.4.0`, the tag should be `ast-py/v0.4.0`.

To deploy:

1. create a PR that sets the version number of the package in `__init__.py` and `docs/conf.py` to what it should be.
2. once the PR has merged, tag the merge commit (for example, `git fetch origin; git tag -m "Python AST 0.4.0" ast-py/v0.4.0 origin/main`).
3. push the tag to this repository (for example, `git push origin ast-py/v0.4.0`).

At this point, the deployment pipeline will take over and deploy the package to PyPI.
You should be able to see the progress [in the Actions tab of this repository](https://github.com/openqasm/openqasm/actions/workflows/deploy-ast.yml).

functions.name: openqasm3.properties.precedence
documentation:
Get the integer value of the precedence level of an expression node.

The actual numeric value has no real meaning and is subject to change
between different versions of the AST and versions of the language.  It is
only intended to be used as a key for comparisons between different
expressions.

The number is such that if an AST node representing expression `A`
contains a subexpression `B`, then on output, `B` needs brackets around
it if its precedence is lower than `A`.  If `A` is a left-associative
(right-associative) binary operator, then its right-hand (left-hand)
subexpression also needs brackets if the precendence of the two are equal.

modules.name: openqasm3._antlr
documentation:
ANTLR-generated files for parsing OpenQASM 3 files.

This package sets up its import contents to be taken from the generated files
whose ANTLR version matches the installed version of the ANTLR runtime.  The
generated files should be placed in directories called `_<major>_<minor>`,
where `major` is 4, and `minor` is the minor version of ANTLR (e.g. if ANTLR
4.10 was used, those files should be in `_4_10`).

The ANTLR files from more than one version of ANTLR can be present at once.  This package will
dynamically load the correct version based on the installed version of the runtime.

variables.name: openqasm3._antlr.RUNTIME_VERSION
documentation: The runtime-detected version of the ANTLR runtime, as a tuple like `sys.version_info`.

classes.name: openqasm3._antlr.ANTLRMetaPathFinder
documentation:
Redirect module/package lookups in `openqasm3.antlr` to the concrete implementations
pre-generated by the ANTLR version that matches the installed version of the runtime.

modules.name: openqasm3
documentation:
# OpenQASM 3 Python reference package

This package contains the reference abstract syntax tree (AST) for representing
OpenQASM 3 programs, tools to parse text into this AST, and tools to manipulate
the AST.

The AST itself is in the {obj}`.ast` module.  There is a reference parser in the
{obj}`.parser` module, which requires the `[parser]` extra to be installed.

With the `[parser]` extra installed, the simplest interface to the parser is
the {obj}`~parser.parse` function.

modules.name: openqasm3._antlr._4_8.qasm3Parser
documentation: Generated from qasm3Parser.g4 by ANTLR 4.8
encoding: utf-8

.. currentmodule:: openqasm3.spec
```

```
.. autodata:: supported_versions
```

modules.name: openqasm3.printer
documentation:
# Generating OpenQASM 3 from an AST Node (`openqasm3.printer`)

```
.. currentmodule:: openqasm3
```

It is often useful to go from the {mod}`AST representation <openqasm3.ast>` of an OpenQASM 3 program
back to the textual language.  The functions and classes described here will do this conversion.

Most uses should be covered by using {func}`dump` to write to an open text stream (an open file, for
example) or {func}`dumps` to produce a string.  Both of these accept {ref}`several keyword arguments
<printer-kwargs>` that control the formatting of the output.

```
.. autofunction:: openqasm3.dump
```

```
.. autofunction:: openqasm3.dumps

```

(printer-kwargs)=

## Controlling the formatting

```
.. currentmodule:: openqasm3.printer
```

The {func}`~openqasm3.dump` and {func}`~openqasm3.dumps` functions both use an internal AST-visitor
class to operate on the AST.  This class actually defines all the formatting options, and can be
used for more low-level operations, such as writing a program piece-by-piece.  This may need access
to the {ref}`printer state <printer-state>`, documented below.

```
.. autoclass:: Printer
    :members:
    :class-doc-from: both
```

For the most complete control, it is possible to subclass this printer and override only the visitor
methods that should be modified.

(printer-state)=

## Reusing the same printer

```
.. currentmodule:: openqasm3.printer
```

If the {class}`Printer` is being reused to write multiple nodes to a single stream, you will also
likely need to access its internal state.  This can be done by manually creating a
{class}`PrinterState` object and passing it in the original call to {meth}`Printer.visit`.  The
state object is mutated by the visit, and will reflect the output state at the end.

```
.. autoclass:: PrinterState
    :members:
```

functions.name: openqasm3.printer.dump
documentation:
Write textual OpenQASM 3 code representing `node` to the open stream `file`.

It is generally expected that `node` will be an instance of {class}`.ast.Program`, but this
does not need to be the case.

For more details on the available keyword arguments, see {ref}`printer-kwargs`.

functions.name: openqasm3.printer.dumps
documentation:
Get a string representation of the OpenQASM 3 code representing `node`.

It is generally expected that `node` will be an instance of {class}`.ast.Program`, but this
does not need to be the case.

For more details on the available keyword arguments, see {ref}`printer-kwargs`.

classes.name: openqasm3.printer.PrinterState

methods.name: openqasm3.printer.PrinterState.increase_scope
documentation:
Use as a context manager to increase the scoping level of this context inside the
resource block.

class_variables.name: openqasm3.printer.PrinterState.current_indent
documentation:
The current indentation level.  This is a non-negative integer; the actual identation string
to be used is defined by the {class}`Printer`.

class_variables.name: openqasm3.printer.PrinterState.skip_next_indent
documentation:
This is used to communicate between the different levels of if-else visitors when emitting
chained `else if` blocks.  The chaining occurs with the next `if` if this is set to
`True`.
State object for the print visitor.  This is mutated during the visit.

classes.name: openqasm3.printer.Printer

methods.name: openqasm3.printer.Printer.__init__
openqasm3.printer.Printer.visit
documentation:
Completely visit a node and all subnodes.  This is the dispatch entry point; this will
automatically result in the correct specialised visitor getting called.

```
param node

The AST node to visit.  Usually this will be an .

type node

.ast.QASMNode

param context

The state object to be used during the visit.  If not given, a default
object will be constructed and used.

type context

PrinterState
```

Internal AST-visitor for writing AST nodes out to a stream as valid OpenQASM 3.

This class can be used directly to write multiple nodes to the same stream, potentially with
some manual control fo the state between them.

If subclassing, generally only the specialised `visit_*` methods need to be overridden.  These
are derived from the base class, and use the name of the relevant {mod}`AST node <.ast>`
verbatim after `visit_`.

modules.name: openqasm3.ast
documentation:
# Abstract Syntax Tree (`openqasm3.ast`)

```
.. currentmodule:: openqasm3.ast
```

The reference abstract syntax tree (AST) for OpenQASM 3 programs.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.Span
documentation:
Start and end line/column in the source file
We use the Antlr convention. The starting line number is 1 and starting column number is 0.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.QASMNode

class_variables.name: openqasm3.ast.QASMNode.span
documentation:
The span(location) of the node in the source code.
Because not all the nodes are generated from source, the span is optional.
To make it easier to write unit test, we exclude span from the generated __eq__().
Base class for all OpenQASM 3 nodes

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.Program
documentation: An entire OpenQASM 3 program represented by a list of top level statements

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.Annotation
documentation: An annotation applied to a statement.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.Statement
documentation: A statement: anything that can appear on its own line

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.CompoundStatement
documentation: A sequence of statements enclosed within an anonymous scope block

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.Include
documentation: An include statement

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ExpressionStatement
documentation: A statement that contains a single expression

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.QubitDeclaration
documentation:
Global qubit declaration

Example:

```
qubit q;
qubit[4] q;

q // <- qubit
4 // <- size
```
Note that QubitDeclaration is not a valid QuantumStatement, because qubits
can only be declared in global scopes, not in gates.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.QuantumGateDefinition
openqasm3.ast.QuantumStatement
documentation: Statements that may appear inside a gate declaration

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ExternDeclaration
documentation:
A extern declaration

Example:

```
extern get_pauli(int[prec]) -> bit[2 * n];

get_pauli  // <- name
int[prec]  // <- classical type
bit[2 * n] // <- return type
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.Expression
documentation: An expression: anything that returns a value

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.Identifier
documentation:
An identifier

Example:

```
q1
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.UnaryExpression
documentation:
A unary expression

Example:

```
~b
!bool
-i
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.BinaryExpression
documentation:
A binary expression

Example:

```
q1 || q2
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.IntegerLiteral
documentation:
An integer literal

Example:

```
1
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.FloatLiteral
documentation:
An real number literal

Example:

```
1.1
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ImaginaryLiteral
documentation:
An real number literal

Example:

```
1.1im
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.BooleanLiteral
documentation:
A boolean expression

Example:

```
true
false
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.BitstringLiteral
documentation:
A literal bitstring value.  The `value` is the numerical value of the
bitstring, and the `width` is the number of digits given.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.DurationLiteral
documentation:
A duration literal

Example:

```
1.0ns
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ArrayLiteral
openqasm3.ast.FunctionCall
documentation:
A function call expression

Example:

```
foo(1)

foo // <- name
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.Cast
documentation:
A cast call expression

Example:

```
counts += int[1](b);
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.DiscreteSet
openqasm3.ast.RangeDefinition
documentation:
Range definition.

Example:

```
1:2
1:1:10
:
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.IndexExpression
documentation:
An index expression.

Example:

```
q[1]
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.IndexedIdentifier
documentation:
An indentifier with index operators, such that it can be used as an
lvalue.  The list of indices is subsequent index brackets, so in:

```
a[{1, 2, 3}][0:1, 0:1]
```

the list of indices will have two elements.  The first will be a
{class}`.DiscreteSet`, and the second will be a list of two
{class}`.RangeDefinition`s.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.Concatenation
documentation:
Concatenation of two registers, for example:

```
a ++ b
a[2:3] ++ a[0:1]
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.QuantumGate
documentation:
Invoking a quantum gate

Example::

: cx\[dur\] 0, 1;

  or

  ctrl @ p(λ) a, b;

  ctrl @ // \<- quantumGateModifier
  p // \<- quantumGateName
  λ // \<- argument
  a, b // \<- qubit

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.QuantumGateModifier
documentation:
A quantum gate modifier

Attributes:

: modifier: 'inv', 'pow', or 'ctrl'
  expression: only pow modifier has expression.

Example:

```
inv @
pow(1./2.)
ctrl
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.QuantumPhase
documentation:
A quantum phase instruction

Example:

```
ctrl @ gphase(λ) a;

ctrl @ // <- quantumGateModifier
λ // <- argument
a // <- qubit
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.QuantumMeasurement
documentation:
A quantum measurement instruction

Example:

```
measure q;
```
Not a full expression because it can only be used in limited contexts.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.QuantumMeasurementStatement
documentation:
Stand-alone statement of a quantum measurement, potentially assigning the
result to a classical variable.  This is not the only statement that
`measure` can appear in (it can also be in classical declaration statements
and returns).
Note that this is not a QuantumStatement because it involves access to
classical bits.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.QuantumBarrier
documentation:
A quantum barrier instruction

Example:

```
barrier q;
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.QuantumReset
documentation:
A reset instruction.

Example:

```
reset q;
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ClassicalArgument
documentation: Classical argument for a gate or subroutine declaration

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ExternArgument
documentation: Classical argument for an extern declaration.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ClassicalDeclaration
documentation:
Classical variable declaration

Example:

```
bit c;
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.IODeclaration
openqasm3.ast.ConstantDeclaration
documentation:
Constant declaration

Example:

```
const int[16] n = 10;
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ClassicalType
documentation: Base class for classical type

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.IntType
documentation:
Node representing a classical `int` (signed integer) type, with an
optional precision.

Example:

> int\[8\]
> int\[16\]

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.UintType
documentation:
Node representing a classical `uint` (unsigned integer) type, with an
optional precision.

Example:

> uint\[8\]
> uint\[16\]

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.FloatType
documentation:
Node representing the classical `float` type, with the particular IEEE-754
floating-point size optionally specified.

Example:

> float\[16\]
> float\[64\]

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ComplexType
documentation:
Complex ClassicalType. Its real and imaginary parts are based on other classical types.

Example:

```
complex[float]
complex[float[32]]
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.AngleType
documentation:
Node representing the classical `angle` type, with an optional precision.

Example:

```
angle[8]
angle[16]
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.BitType
documentation:
Node representing the classical `bit` type, with an optional size.

Example:

```
bit[8]
creg[8]
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.BoolType
documentation: Leaf node representing the Boolean classical type.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ArrayType
documentation:
Type of arrays that include allocation of the storage.

This is generally any array declared as a standard statement, but not
arrays declared by being arguments to subroutines.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ArrayReferenceType
openqasm3.ast.DurationType
documentation: Leaf node representing the `duration` type.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.StretchType
documentation: Leaf node representing the `stretch` type.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.CalibrationGrammarDeclaration
documentation:
Calibration grammar declaration

Example:

```
defcalgrammar "openpulse";
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.CalibrationStatement
openqasm3.ast.CalibrationDefinition
openqasm3.ast.SubroutineDefinition
openqasm3.ast.QuantumArgument
documentation: Quantum argument for a subroutine declaration

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ReturnStatement
documentation:
Classical or quantum return statement

Example:

```
return measure q;

return a + b
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.BreakStatement
documentation:
Break statement

Example:

```
break;
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ContinueStatement
documentation:
Continue statement

Example:

```
continue;
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.EndStatement
documentation:
End statement

Example:

```
end;
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.BranchingStatement
openqasm3.ast.WhileLoop
openqasm3.ast.ForInLoop
openqasm3.ast.SwitchStatement
documentation:
A switch-case statement.

The literal cases are stored in the `cases` dictionary, in declaration
order.  Python's `dict` guarantees (for all supported versions of Python)
that the iteration order will be insertion order, so this field *is*
ordered.

The default case, if any, is in the `default` attribute.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.DelayInstruction
documentation:
Delay instruction

Example:

```
delay[start_stretch] $0;
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.Box
openqasm3.ast.DurationOf
documentation:
Duration Of

Example:

```
durationof({x $0;})
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.SizeOf
documentation: ``sizeof`` an array's dimensions.

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.AliasStatement
documentation:
Alias statement

Example:

```
let a = qubits[0];
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.ClassicalAssignment
documentation:
Classical assignment

Example:

```
a[0] = 1;
```

classes.name: openqasm3.ast.Pragma
documentation:
Pragma
Example:

```
#pragma val1 val2 val3

modules.name: openqasm3.visitor
documentation:
# AST Visitors and Transformers (`openqasm3.visitor`)

Implementation of an example AST visitor {obj}`~QASMVisitor`, which can be
inherited from to make generic visitors of the reference AST.  Deriving from
this is {obj}`~QASMTransformer`, which is an example of how the AST can be
manipulated.

classes.name: openqasm3.visitor.QASMVisitor

methods.name: openqasm3.visitor.QASMVisitor.visit
documentation: Visit a node.

methods.name: openqasm3.visitor.QASMVisitor.generic_visit
documentation: Called if no explicit visitor function exists for a node.

A node visitor base class that walks the abstract syntax tree and calls a
visitor function for every node found.  This function may return a value
which is forwarded by the `visit` method.

Modified from the implementation in ast.py in the Python standard library.
We added the context argument to the visit method. It allows the visitor
to hold temporary state while visiting the nodes.

The optional context argument in visit/generic_visit methods can be used to hold temporary
information that we do not want to hold in either the AST or the visitor themselves.

classes.name: openqasm3.visitor.QASMTransformer
documentation:
A {class}`QASMVisitor` subclass that walks the abstract syntax tree and
allows modification of nodes.

Modified from the implementation in ast.py in the Python standard library

modules.name: openqasm3.parser
documentation:
# Parser (`openqasm3.parser`)

Tools for parsing OpenQASM 3 programs into the {obj}`reference AST <openqasm3.ast>`.

The quick-start interface is simply to call `openqasm3.parse`:

```
.. currentmodule:: openqasm3
```

```
.. autofunction:: openqasm3.parse
```

The rest of this module provides some lower-level internals of the parser.

```
.. currentmodule:: openqasm3.parser
```

```
.. autofunction:: span
```

```
.. autofunction:: add_span
```

```
.. autofunction:: combine_span
```

```
.. autofunction:: get_span
```

```
.. autoclass:: QASMNodeVisitor
```

functions.name: openqasm3.parser.parse
documentation:
Parse a complete OpenQASM 3 program from a string.

```
param input

A string containing a complete OpenQASM 3 program.

param permissive

A Boolean controlling whether ANTLR should attempt to
recover from incorrect input or not.  Defaults to False; if set to
True, the reference AST produced may be invalid if ANTLR emits any
warning messages during its parsing phase.

return

A complete  node.
```

functions.name: openqasm3.parser.get_span
documentation: Get the span of a node

functions.name: openqasm3.parser.add_span
documentation: Set the span of a node and return the node

functions.name: openqasm3.parser.combine_span
documentation: Combine two spans and return the combined one

functions.name: openqasm3.parser.span
documentation: Function decorator to automatic attach span to nodes for visit* methods.

classes.name: openqasm3.parser.QASM3ParsingError
documentation:
An error raised by the AST visitor during the AST-generation phase.  This is raised in cases where the
given program could not be correctly parsed.
